failure_surface: Output spreadsheet contains excessive NaN/missing values after column filtering operation, indicating data loss or misalignment during transformation
root_cause: Using pandas' to_excel() function overwrites all existing formatting and cannot properly preserve complex column structures; the subsequent openpyxl formatting preservation code loads workbooks but never saves the modified workbook, rendering the preservation effort ineffective
minimal_fix_hypothesis: For tasks involving column selection/filtering while preserving formatting, use openpyxl for direct worksheet manipulation instead of pandas DataFrame conversion, and ensure any modified workbook objects are explicitly saved back to disk
evidence: Original shape (4, 1592) reduced to (4, 1177) but output displays excessive NaN values across cells
Code uses df.to_excel() which discards formatting and can corrupt structured data layout
Formatting preservation block loads wb_original and wb_new but modifies neither workbook before attempting to save
wb_new = load_workbook(output_path) opens the already-saved pandas output rather than applying changes to the in-memory filtered result
Sample columns show structured nested path notation (e.g., 'organicResults/0/description') which pandas may not interpret correctly during export

memory_items.title: Pandas Export Formatting Loss
memory_items.lesson: pandas.DataFrame.to_excel() does not preserve cell formatting, column widths, merged cells, or formulas from source files
memory_items.generalization: When formatting preservation is required, prefer openpyxl for read/write cycles instead of converting through pandas DataFrames

memory_items.title: Workbook Modification Must Be Saved
memory_items.lesson: Loading a workbook object and making modifications requires calling workbook.save() on the modified object to persist changes
memory_items.generalization: Always verify that any workbook loaded for editing is explicitly saved to a path after modifications are applied

memory_items.title: Column Filtering Without Pandas
memory_items.lesson: Column deletion based on header patterns can be done directly in openpyxl by iterating through ws.columns and removing matching indices
memory_items.generalization: Use openpyxl's worksheet.iter_cols() or ws.delete_cols() methods for column-level manipulations when format integrity matters

edits.content:
### Formatting Preservation During Column Operations

- **Avoid pandas.to_excel() for formatted files**: Using pandas' to_excel() will overwrite cell formatting, column widths, formulas, and merged cell structures. For tasks requiring format retention, perform read/edit/write cycles entirely within openpyxl.

- **Ensure workbook modifications are persisted**: When loading a workbook with openpyxl.load_workbook(), any modifications to the workbook object must be followed by an explicit call to `workbook.save(filepath)`. Loading a workbook without saving makes all changes transient.

- **Prefer direct worksheet manipulation for column filtering**: Instead of converting to pandas and filtering, iterate through worksheet columns using `iter_cols()` or identify column indices by header value, then use `ws.delete_column()` or build a new worksheet copying only desired columns while preserving cell metadata.
